reasoning: The PHPCS nonce suppression at lines 178-185 of Search.php is not a real CSRF vulnerability. Three independent reasons converge: (1) The endpoint is intentionally public. It is registered on 'wc_ajax_dgwt_wcas_ajax_search' (WooCommerce AJAX, publicly reachable) AND on 'wp_ajax_nopriv_dgwt_wcas_ajax_search', meaning it is explicitly designed to work without any login. Nonce protection presupposes an authenticated user whose session can be hijacked — that precondition does not hold here. (2) No state change occurs. getSearchResults() is a pure read: it queries WooCommerce products (categories, tags, post content) and echoes JSON. CSRF is only harmful when a cross-site request causes a server-side state change on behalf of the victim. Reading a search index changes nothing. (3) The returned data is public. The endpoint surfaces the same product names, categories, and tags that any anonymous visitor sees via the front-end search widget. Even if an attacker could read the JSON response (which browser Same-Origin Policy prevents in the absence of CORS headers — none are set by this plugin), they would learn only public catalog data. The finding's claim that an attacker can 'reveal returned data via CORS or redirect' is false: no Access-Control-Allow-Origin header is emitted and no redirect mechanic exists in the response path. The sanitize_text_field() calls on $_REQUEST['dgwt_wcas_keyword'] and $_REQUEST['s'] (lines 180, 183) mean the keyword input is properly sanitized before use. The phpcs:disable comment is a code-style suppression appropriate for a public, authentication-free search endpoint, not evidence of a missing security control.
